author: @recordationperioperativein5238
text: Iron powder burns in air and is the key element in winter hand warmers; these give off heat for many hours. It would be cool to reset them somehow but getting iron oxide back to tiny particular iron would seem difficult.

author: @gadgehamilton3134
text: Magnesium is lighter, and more exothermic, both things that would make it a better solid fuel. My question i geuss would be why would you want to use buring metal as a fuel at all

replies.author: @Biggles732
replies.text: Also magnesium will burn in nitrogen gas I believe. Also the metal oxides leave behind a vacuum but only as far as 22% of the volume would be concerned for iron , but when it also removes the nitrogen............ . So perhaps not useful for internal combustion engines thet rely on expansion of gas.

author: @FranckG31
text: This is not an energy source. Only a mean of storage/transportation of energy. Can't use it in an engine....

replies.author: @ATxPrime
replies.text:
Not with THAT attitude 😂

Jokes aside, why would this be any harder to use in an engine than shoveling coal was 200 years ago?

Yes, it's significantly less efficient than other modernized models, but with 10 or 20 years of development and iteration a lot can change.

replies.author: @Philip-hv2kc
replies.text: The iron powder, coal dust , flour .....have been used in test engines but durability is the next question. Not sure if magnesium could be used on account it would react with the nitrogen but then it's just a matter of tuning the stoichiometric ratios probably.

author: @johnlshilling1446
text:
It's a one-way trip. The energy required to convert the rust back into iron would be much more than you'd get from burning the iron powder. 
This is nothing more than a curiosity. And we're enjoying the Media Hype phase.
